---
title: "Cumulative injustice"
subtitle: "Exposure to unjust policing across the life course"
author:
  - name: Thiago R. Oliveira
    affiliation: University of Manchester
  - name: Jonathan Jackson
    affiliation: London School of Economics
  - name: David S. Kirk
    affiliation: University of Pennsylvania
format:
  revealjs: 
    theme: serif
    footer: |
      <div class="footer-logos">
      <img src="files/images/TAB_black.png" alt="UoM">
      </div>
    css: files/styles.css
    incremental: false
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.4.0/css/all.min.css">
    width: 1200
    height: 800
execute:
  fig-format: svg 
bibliography: files/bib.bib
mouse-wheel: true
history: false
---



```{r, echo = F}
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(showtext)
library(systemfonts)
options(scipen=999)
options(knitr.kable.NA = '')
#font_add_google("EB Garamond", "ebgaramond")
#showtext_auto()

theme_set(
  theme_void(base_size = 14) +
    theme(
      plot.background  = element_rect(fill = NA, colour = NA),
      panel.background = element_rect(fill = NA, colour = NA)
    )
)

# Load your data
load("files/results.RData")
```

## Teachable moments as static encounters

&nbsp;

Most criminological research on procedural justice

* Treats encounters as isolated moments 
  * Police-citizen encounters <i class="fa fa-car-on"></i>
  * Police stops <i class="fa fa-people-robbery"></i>
  * Highly publicized incidents <i class="fa fa-video"></i>
  
> "Each of these police-citizen contacts is potentially a 'teachable moment' about policing for both citizens and police" [@tyler2014street, p. 752]


## Teachable moments as dynamic exposures
  
&nbsp;

> Encounters are not evaluated on a blank slate <i class="fa fa-file"></i>

&nbsp;

$\leadsto$ Experiences with law enforcement accumulate over time <i class="fa fa-signal"></i>

$\leadsto$ Encounters are embedded within broader trajectories of repeated experiences and neighbourhood socialisation <i class="fa fa-chart-line"></i> 


# Theoretical insights from criminological theory

## Life-course theory of cumulative disadvantage

> "The idea of cumulative disadvantage draws on a **dynamic** conceptualization of social control over the life course, integrated with the one theoretical perspective in criminology that is inherently developmental in nature---**labeling theory**" [@sampson1997life, pp. 1-3]

* A developmental theory focused on dynamic processes <i class="fa fa-chart-line"></i>

* Cumulative disadvantage mechanism <i class="fa fa-signal"></i>

* Identity-based mechanisms, turning points, and redirected trajectories <i class="fa fa-arrow-right-arrow-left"></i>


## Legal cynicism theory

> "Legal cynicism refers to a cultural orientation in which the law and the agents of its enforcement, such as the police and courts, are viewed as illegitimate, unresponsive, and ill equipped to ensure public safety" [@kirk2011cultural, p. 1191]

&nbsp;

* Builds on the life-course theory of cumulative disadvantage <i class="fa fa-chart-line"></i> [@sampson1997life]

* **Neighborhood socialization**: police officers are there to protect others against us <i class="fa fa-arrows-down-to-people"></i>

* Labeling mechanism: individuals labeled as *others* and *excluded* from the body politic <i class="fa fa-ban"></i>


# Our proposal

## A developmental model of repeated teachable moments

> <i class="fa fa-signal"></i> Reframe experiences with unjust policing as cumulative, developmental, and contextual

&nbsp;

<div style="margin-top: -10px">  
Each individual event remains a teachable moment, but they are embedded within **trajectories of exposure across the life course**
</div>
<div style="font-size: 0.75em">  
  * <i class="fa fa-car-on"></i> Direct exposures
  * <i class="fa fa-network-wired"></i> Network exposures
  * <i class="fa fa-people-roof"></i> Neighborhood exposures
  * <i class="fa fa-house-chimney"></i> Intergenerational experiences
</div>
  

## Cumulative injustice

Encounters communicate **inclusion** or **exclusion** --- but we need to situate those signals within the temporal, cultural, and structural contexts in which they are lived

&nbsp;

> “I was eight when I first got stopped. (...) Thirteen, fourteen, that’s when I get stopped at least three times a week” [@haldipur2018no, p. 43]

&nbsp;

Accumulated experiences of unjust policing across the life course <br> $\Rightarrow$ socialization context of **cumulative injustice** <i class="fa fa-arrow-trend-up"></i>

# Some empirical evidence

## Some empirical evidence

> Average developmental trajectory of legal cynicism in the UK^[$NT = 28,914; N=10,472; SD(Intercept)=1.526; SD(age)=0.023$]

<div style="background:none; margin-top:-40px; text-align:center;">
  <object type="image/svg+xml" 
          data="files/plot_empty.svg"
          style="width:75%; height:auto;"></object>
</div>

## Some empirical evidence

> Effects of the number of police stops in adolescence on developmental trajectories of legal cynicism

<div style="background:none; margin-top:-20px; text-align:center;">
  <object type="image/svg+xml" 
          data="files/plot_full.svg"
          style="width:85%; height:auto;"></object>
</div>

## Concluding remarks

&nbsp;

* From **isolated encounters** to **cumulative trajectories**  

* **Cumulative injustice**: A research agenda focused on cumulative exposures to unjust policing across the life course
  * Dose–response
  * Timing
  * Trajectories

$\leadsto$ A **life-course approach to procedural justice theory**


# Thank you!

<br><br>

<div style="text-align:left; margin-left:200px; font-size:1.2em; line-height:2;">
  <i class="fa fa-envelope"></i> 
  <a href="mailto:thiago.oliveira@manchester.ac.uk">thiago.oliveira@manchester.ac.uk</a> <br>

  <i class="fa fa-home"></i> 
  <a href="https://www.thiagoroliveira.com">ThiagoROliveira.com</a> <br>

  <i class="fa fa-link"></i> 
  <a href="https://bsky.app/profile/oliveiratr.bsky.social">\@oliveiratr.bsky.social</a>
</div>

# Data

> British Cohort Study

* Following the lives of 17,000 people born in the UK in 1970
* 12 sweeps of data collection, from infancy to age 46

&nbsp;

**Measures**

* Any experiences of **police stops** between ages 10 and 16
* *Number* of experiences of **police stops followed by a warning** between ages 10 and 16
* **Legal cynicism** measured at ages 16, 26, 30, and 34

## Analytic strategy

> Growth curve model

&nbsp;

* Developmental trajectories of legal cynicism from age 16 through 34
* Influence of the *number* of police stops followed by a warning between ages 10 and 16 on those developmental trajectories
  * Effects on the initial state of the trajectories
  * Effects on the shape of the trajectories

## Analytic strategy

* Binary outcome: Legal cynicism at ages 16, 26, 30, 34
* Time specification: age and age squared (*quadratic growth curve*)
* Main explanatory variable: number of police stops followed by a warning between ages 10 and 16 (square rooted)
* Covariates:
<div style="font-size: 0.75em;">
  * demographics (sex, ethnicity, socioeconomic status)
  * psycho-social confounders at age 10 (impulsivity, aggressiveness, learning scores)
  * behavioural confounders at age 10 (bullying behaviour, violent behaviour)
</div>

$\leadsto$ Multilevel growth curve model specified with a logistic function link and interaction terms

## References
